narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA right around the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 51%, tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the average 5.53 and stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting a bar failure. Your Year 1 decile of 2nd, also below the typical 5.71, added a smaller nudge in the same concerning direction. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 played a significant role in countering that risk, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other factor. Your middle family income bracket further helped by suggesting a supportive background compared to others, while your planned full-time enrollment aligned with the norm for 92.7% of students and offered a modest boost toward passing. Even your LSAT score of 14.50, though well below the average 36.70, had only a minor favorable influence in this case. Overall, your situation compares to typical students by having average GPA and income but lower early academic deciles and LSAT, which outweighed the positives here. We know this outcome can feel disheartening, especially given the unique challenges many Black women face in competitive academic pipelines. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your full story. You're capable of great things—keep pushing, and many doors will open.
